title: Go Mutex
content:
### Mutex (Mutual Exclusion)

`sync.Mutex` provides mutual exclusion to protect shared resources.

### Basic Mutex

```go
var mu sync.Mutex
var counter int

func increment() {
    mu.Lock()
    counter++
    mu.Unlock()
}
```

### Always Use defer

```go
func increment() {
    mu.Lock()
    defer mu.Unlock()
    counter++
    // more operations...
}
```

### RWMutex (Read-Write Mutex)

```go
var rw sync.RWMutex
var data map[string]int

func read(key string) int {
    rw.RLock()
    defer rw.RUnlock()
    return data[key]
}

func write(key string, value int) {
    rw.Lock()
    defer rw.Unlock()
    data[key] = value
}
```

RWMutex allows multiple concurrent readers but exclusive writers.

### Mutex vs RWMutex

| Aspect | Mutex | RWMutex |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Read concurrency | 1 reader | N readers |
| Write concurrency | 1 writer | 1 writer |
| Overhead | Lower | Higher |
| Best for | Mixed read/write | Read-heavy workloads |

### Common Pitfalls

```go
// DEADLOCK: Locking already locked mutex (non-reentrant)
func main() {
    var mu sync.Mutex
    mu.Lock()
    mu.Lock() // deadlock!
}

// FORGOT UNLOCK: Use defer to ensure unlock
func process() {
    mu.Lock()
    if someCondition {
        return // forgets to unlock!
    }
    mu.Unlock()
}
```

### Mutex with Struct

```go
type SafeCounter struct {
    mu    sync.Mutex
    value int
}

func (c *SafeCounter) Increment() {
    c.mu.Lock()
    defer c.mu.Unlock()
    c.value++
}

func (c *SafeCounter) Value() int {
    c.mu.Lock()
    defer c.mu.Unlock()
    return c.value
}
```

> **Key Point:** A Mutex must not be copied after first use. Always pass pointer to a struct containing a Mutex. Use `defer mu.Unlock()` immediately after `Lock()`.

quiz.question: What happens if a goroutine locks an already locked mutex?
